page: Amazon Web Services x1e.32xlarge is a memory-optimized instance running on the Xen hypervisor. It features 128 vCPUs and 64 physical cores powered by Intel Xeon E7-8880 v3 processors operating at 2.3 GHz. The architecture is x86_64, with a 45 MB L3 cache. The instance is equipped with 3904.0 GB of system memory, yielding 30.5 GB of RAM per physical core. Local storage consists of 3840 GB of NVMe SSD capacity, and the baseline network bandwidth is 25 Gbps.

Benchmark data reveals a clear tradeoff between single-core compute and memory throughput. Single-core performance is low, with stress-ng single-core and Geekbench single-core scores falling into the bottom 10%. Conversely, memory bandwidth is strong, ranking in the top 25% for both cached small-block reads and uncached large-block read/write operations. Multi-core performance is average, as shown by stress-ng multi-core and Geekbench multi-core benchmarks. Passmark CPU and extended instruction tests rank in the top 25%, while Passmark memory scores are weak, placing in the bottom 25%.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the hardware profile represents a high resource density configuration designed for memory-bound workloads rather than raw single-threaded CPU speed. The massive memory-to-vCPU ratio and local NVMe SSD storage make this instance suitable for large in-memory databases, memory-intensive enterprise applications, and multi-threaded data processing tasks that can leverage the 128 vCPUs despite the lower single-core clock speeds.
description: Amazon Web Services x1e.32xlarge is a memory-optimized instance featuring 128 vCPUs and 64 physical cores powered by Intel Xeon E7-8880 v3 processors at 2.3 GHz. Built on the x86_64 architecture, it provides 3904.0 GB of RAM, 3840 GB of local NVMe SSD storage, and 25 Gbps baseline network bandwidth. Benchmark results indicate a significant tradeoff: single-core performance is poor, ranking in the bottom 10% for Geekbench and stress-ng, whereas memory bandwidth is strong, placing in the top 25% for both cached and uncached operations. Multi-core performance and database operations remain average. This high-density memory configuration is designed for memory-intensive workloads, such as large-scale in-memory databases and enterprise applications, where memory capacity and multi-threaded throughput are prioritized over single-threaded CPU speed.
